title: Chicken & Mushroom Pie

overview.description: Tender chicken and mushrooms in a creamy herb sauce, baked beneath a golden pastry lid. Classic British comfort food that's perfect for batch cooking.

method: Heat the butter in a large saucepan over a medium heat.
Cook the onions for 8–10 minutes until softened.
Add the mushrooms and cook until their liquid has evaporated and they begin to brown.
Stir in the garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Add the chicken and cook until lightly coloured.
Sprinkle over the flour and stir well for 2 minutes.
Gradually add the chicken stock, stirring continuously until smooth.
Add the cream, Dijon mustard and thyme.
Simmer gently for 15–20 minutes until the sauce has thickened and the chicken is cooked.
Stir through the chopped parsley and allow the filling to cool slightly.
Transfer the filling to a large pie dish.
Top with the prepared pastry and trim the edges.
Cut a small steam hole in the centre.
Bake at 180°C fan (200°C conventional) for 30–35 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and crisp.
Allow to stand for 10 minutes before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Chicken and mushroom pie has been a staple of British home cooking for generations, combining simple ingredients into one of the country's favourite comfort foods.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make a creamy pie filling, thicken a sauce with flour and bake a crisp pastry lid.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A classic freezer-friendly comfort meal that's ideal for Saturday batch cooking and makes excellent leftovers.
chapman-tips: Chicken thighs stay much juicier than breast meat in pies.
Cool the filling slightly before adding the pastry to help keep it crisp.
Chestnut mushrooms provide a deeper flavour than white mushrooms.
Leftover roast chicken can be used instead—simply stir it into the finished sauce and reduce the simmering time.
notes.batch-cooking: Freeze either the finished baked pie or an assembled unbaked pie for up to three months.
notes.leftovers: Reheat in the oven to keep the pastry crisp.
